properties.name: UGIB 2020 Last Estimated Position (LEP)
properties.publishedBy: Bobby Ulich, Richard Godfrey, Victor Iannello, Andrew Banks

citation.label: 'The Final Resting Place of MH370' (UGIB 2020), pp.52, 57: 'the LEP is S34.2342° E93.7875°'

properties.methodology: SATCOM BTO/BFO route fitting over all candidate LNAV routes, combined with fuel-range/endurance, debris-drift and aerial-search probabilities; best-fit route is LNAV 180.0 deg true from waypoint BEDAX toward the South Pole at FL390/LRC, impact on the 7th arc at the BEDAX longitude
properties.note: On the 7th arc inside the 2014-17 ATSB corridor and the 2018 OI area, but UGIB (p.52) identify unscanned terrain incl. a steep slope ~20 NM south of the LEP. The point anchors the Ocean Infinity 2025-26 search zone (33S-36S).

properties.name: CSIRO drift-analysis most-likely location (Part III)
properties.publishedBy: CSIRO Oceans and Atmosphere (David Griffin, Peter Oke), for the ATSB

citation.label: 'The search for MH370 and ocean surface drift - Part III', report EP174155, pp.6, 28: 'the location of the aircraft, with unprecedented precision and certainty. This location is 35.6°S, 92.8°E.'

properties.methodology: Forward drift modelling of recovered debris and of objects in March 2014 Pleiades satellite imagery (image PHR4), constrained by the 2014 surface search, absence of Australian-coast debris, and arrival timing of African-shore debris
properties.note: The report also allows lower-likelihood west-of-arc alternatives near 34.7S 92.6E and 35.3S 91.8E. The 35.6S region was covered by Ocean Infinity in 2018 without a find; parts are being re-examined in the wider 2025-26 zone.

properties.name: ATSB First Principles Review remaining priority area (indicative centre)
properties.publishedBy: ATSB First Principles Review expert panel (with DST Group, CSIRO, Boeing, Inmarsat, DCA Malaysia and others)

citation.label: ATSB, 'MH370 - First Principles Review' (AE-2014-054), pp.3, 23: ~25,000 km2 remaining high-probability area between latitudes 32.5S and 36S along the 7th arc, within ~25 NM (99% of DST Group paths)

properties.methodology: Expert review combining DST Group Bayesian SATCOM analysis, completed-search elimination (high confidence the 120,000 km2 area does not contain the aircraft), end-of-flight/flap analysis (impact within ~25 NM of arc) and CSIRO drift results (region near 35S most consistent)
properties.note: DERIVED CENTRE, not a published coordinate: the FPR publishes only the area bounds; lat is the midpoint of 32.5S-36S and lon is the repo arc-model longitude at that latitude (see meta.notes). Ocean Infinity searched this 25,000 km2 area first in 2018 without a find.

properties.name: Godfrey GDTAAA/WSPR candidate site

citation.label: 'MH370 Search Area (Updated)', mh370search.com, 22 Dec 2023: 'The WSPR search area is a circle with a 30 km radius centred on 29.128°S 99.934°E at a depth of 3,750 m'

properties.methodology: WSPR-based (Weak Signal Propagation Reporter): claimed detection and tracking of the aircraft via anomalies in amateur-radio propagation links (GDTAAA software), cross-referenced with Inmarsat arcs, fuel and drift analyses. DISPUTED METHODOLOGY: the physical basis (aircraft scatter detectable in global WSPR logs) is rejected by several MH370 analysts, including UGIB co-author Victor Iannello
properties.note: About 43 NM east of the 7th arc at 29.1S per the repo arc model - outside every searched corridor; also lies in the latitude band contra-indicated by the CSIRO drift analysis (impact 'likely not north of 32S'). An earlier GDTAAA preliminary finding (30 Nov 2021) was 33.177S 95.300E, 6 NM east of the arc; the 29.128S location is the later refinement publicised 2021-2023. The full WSPR/GDTAAA candidate track is plotted as the wspr-gdtaaa-2023 Epoch 3 reconstruction. Still unsearched after the 2025-26 Ocean Infinity campaign, which targeted the 33-36S zone and ended Jan 2026 without a find (Godfrey's 2023 paper claims ~46% of his 70x40 NM crash area was previously searched).

properties.name: Inmarsat example-path terminus ('hotspot', Ashton et al. 2015)
properties.publishedBy: Inmarsat (Chris Ashton, Alan Shuster Bruce, Gary Colledge, Mark Dickinson)

citation.label: Ashton et al. (2015), 'The Search for MH370', Journal of Navigation 68(1), Table 9 final row 00:19:29 (p.20) and conclusions (p.22): 'a last contact location of 34.7°S and 93.0°E'

properties.methodology: BTO/BFO example reconstruction: constant 450 kt ground-speed route crossing the 19:41 arc at 0N, matching BFO within 2 Hz at the five post-19:41 signalling epochs; the paper stresses 'significant uncertainty in the final location'
properties.note: ~9 NM west of the 7th arc at 34.7S per the repo arc model, inside the ATSB 120,000 km2 corridor searched 2014-17. Widely reported in 2015 media (e.g. The Economist) as the Inmarsat 'hotspot'.

properties.name: CAPTIO 2022 northernmost Point of Impact

citation.label: Blelly & Marchand (CAPTIO v4a, 2022), 'Analysis of Flight MH370', Executive Summary p.1: 'The northernmost probable point of impact (POI) ... is located at [35 deg 39' S; 93 deg 01' E]'; crash zone -35.6650 S 93.0130 E to -36.0269 S 93.0364 E (sections 2.8-2.9)

properties.methodology: Piloted-flight reconstruction: constant FL300 / Mach 0.706 (ICAO holding speed) southern leg fitting all 7 arcs plus 3 extra Inmarsat constraints (18:28 BTO + 18:40/23:14 BFO), followed by a manual engine shutdown, APU-powered glide and controlled ditching south of the 7th arc. The POI is the northern end of a ~22 NM crash-zone segment; the reconstructed track is the captio-2022 Epoch 3 path.
properties.note: The terminus of the CAPTIO reconstruction, ~55 NM SW of the UGIB LEP. Because CAPTIO model a powered glide PAST the 7th arc, the POI lies ~45 NM beyond (SW of) the arc - outside the classic 'within ~25 NM' constraint. Much of the surrounding 33-36 S band was covered by the failed 2025-26 Ocean Infinity search; CAPTIO propose a specific southern extension (see the captio-blelly-marchand-proposed search zone) they argue was not fully scanned.
